title: Matthew V. 17.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Think not that I am come to destroy the Law or the Prophets.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, who suspected this? or who accused Him, that He should make a defense against this charge?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since surely from what had gone beforeno such suspicion was generated.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to command men to be meek, and gentle, and merciful, and pure in heart, and to strive for righteousness, indicated no such design, but rather altogether the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore then can He have said this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not at random, nor vainly: but inasmuch as He was proceeding to ordain commandments greater than those of old, saying, “It was said to them of old time, Thou shalt not kill;but I say unto you, Be not even angry;” and to mark out a way for a kind of divine and heavenly conversation;in order that the strangeness thereof might not disturb the souls of the hearers, nor dispose them quite to mutiny against what He said He used this means of setting them right beforehand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For although they fulfilled not the law, yet nevertheless they were possessed with much conscientious regard to it; and whilst they were annulling it every day by their deeds, the letters thereof they would have remain unmoved, and that no one should add anything more to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, they bore with their rulers adding thereto, not however for the better, but for the worse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For so they used to set aside the honor due to our parents by additions of their own, and very many others also of the matters enjoined them, they would free themselves ofby these unseasonable additions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, since Christ in the first place was not of the sacredotal tribe, and next, the things which He was about to introduce were a sort of addition, not however lessening, but enhancing virtue; He knowing beforehand that both these circumstances would trouble them, before He wrote in their mind those wondrous laws, casts out that which was sure to be harboring there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what was it that was harboring there, and making an obstacle?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They thought that He, thus speaking, did so with a view to the abrogation of the ancient institutions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This suspicion therefore He heals; nor here only doth He so, but elsewhere also again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, since they accounted Him no less than an adversary of God, from this sort of reason, namely, His not keeping the sabbath; He, to heal such their suspicion, there also again sets forth His pleas, of which some indeed were proper to Himself; as when He saith, “My Father worketh, and I work;”but some had in them much condescension, as when He brings forward the sheep lost on the sabbath day,and points out that the law is disturbed for its preservation, and makes mention again of circumcision, as having this same effect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore we see also that He often speaks words somewhat beneath Him, to remove the semblance of His being an adversary of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He who had raised thousands of the dead with a word only, when He was calling Lazarus, added also a prayer; and then, lest this should make Him appear less than Him that begat Him, He, to correct this suspicion, added, “I said these things, because of the people which standeth by, that they may believe that thou hast sent me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And neither doth He work all things as one who acted by His own power, that He might thoroughly correct their weakness; nor doth He all things with prayer, lest He should leave matter of evil suspicion to them that should follow, as though He were without strength or power: but He mingles the latter with the former, and those again with these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Neither doth He this indiscriminately, but with His own proper wisdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For while He doeth the greater works authoritatively, in the less He looks up unto Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when absolving sins, and revealing His secrets, and opening Paradise, and driving away devils, and cleansing lepers, and bridling death, and raising the dead by thousands, He did all by way of command: but when, what was much less than these, He was causing many loaves to spring forth out of few, then He looked up to Heaven: signifying that not through weakness He doth this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He who could do the greater with authority, how in the lesser could He need prayer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as I was saying, He doeth this to silence their shamelessness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The same reckoning, then, I bid thee make of His words also, when thou hearest Him speak lowly things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many in truth are the causes both for words and for actions of that cast: as, for instance, that He might not be supposed alien from God; His instructing and waiting on all men; His teaching humility; His being encompassed with flesh; the Jews’ inability to hear all at once; His teaching us to utter no high word of ourselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause many times, having in His own person said much that is lowly of Himself, the great things He leaves to be said by others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He Himself indeed, reasoning with the Jews, said, “Before Abraham was, I Am:”but His disciple not thus, but, “In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, that He Himself made Heaven, and earth, and sea, and all things visible and invisible, in His own person He nowhere expressly said: but His disciple, speaking plainly out, and suppressing nothing, affirms this once, twice, yea often: writing that “all things were made by Him;” and, “without Him was not one thing made;” and, He was in the world, and the world was made by Him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why marvel, if others have said greater things of Him than He of Himself; since (what is more) in many cases, what He showed forth by His deeds, by His words He uttered not openly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus that it was Himself who made mankind He showed clearly even by that blind man; but when He was speaking of our formation at the beginning, He said not, “I made,” but “He who made them, made them male and female.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, that He created the world and all things therein, He demonstrated by the fishes, by the wine, by the loaves, by the calm in the sea, by the sunbeam which He averted on the Cross; and by very many things besides: but in words He hath nowhere said this plainly, though His disciples are continually declaring it, both John, and Paul, and Peter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they who night and day hear Him discourse, and see Him work marvels; to whom He explained many things in private, and gave so great power as even to raise the dead; whom He made so perfect, as to forsake all things for Him: if even they, after so great virtue and self-denial, had not strength to bear it all, before the supply of the Spirit; how could the people of the Jews, being both void of understanding, and far behind such excellency, and only by hazard present when He did or said anything, how could they have been persuaded but that He was alien from the God of all, unless he had practised such great condescension throughout?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For on this account we see that even when He was abrogating the sabbath, He did not as of set purpose bring in such His legislation, but He puts together many and various pleas of defense.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if, when He was about to cause one commandment to cease, He used so much reserve in His language,that He might not startle the hearers; much more, when adding to the law, entire as it was, another entire code of laws, did He require much management and attention, not to alarm those who were then hearing Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this same cause, neither do we find Him teaching everywhere clearly concerning His own Godhead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if His adding to the law was sure to perplex them so greatly, much more His declaring Himself God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore many things are uttered by Him, far below His proper dignity, and here when He is about to proceed upon His addition to the law, He hath used abundance for correction beforehand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For neither was it once only that He said, “I do not abrogate the law,” but He both repeated it again, and added another and a greater thing; in that, to the words, “Think not that I am come to destroy,” He subjoined, “I am not come to destroy, but to fulfill.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now this not only obstructs the obstinacy of the Jews, but stops also the mouths of those heretics,who say that the old covenant is of the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if Christ came to destroy his tyranny, how is this covenant not only not destroyed, but even fulfilled by Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not only, “I do not destroy it;” though this had been enough; but “I even fulfill it:” which are the words of one so far from opposing himself, as to be even establishing it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how, one may ask, did He not destroy it? in what way did He rather fulfill either the law or the prophets?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The prophets He fulfilled, inasmuch as He confirmed by His actions all that had been said concerning Him; wherefore also the evangelist used to say in each case, “That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Both when He was born,and when the children sung that wondrous hymn to Him, and when He sat on the ass,and in very many more instances He worked this same fulfillment: all which things must have been unfulfilled, if He had not come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the law He fulfilled, not in one way only, but in a second and third also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In one way, by transgressing none of the precepts of the law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that He did fulfill it all, hear what He saith to John, “For thus it becometh us to fulfill all righteousness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to the Jews also He said, “Which of you convinceth me of sin.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to His disciples again, “The prince of this world cometh, and findeth nothing in me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the prophet too from the first had said that “He did no sin.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then was one sense in which He fulfilled it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Another, that He did the same through us also; for this is the marvel, that He not only Himself fulfilled it, but He granted this to us likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which thing Paul also declaring said, “Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he said also, that “He judged sin in the flesh, that the righteousness of the law might be fulfilled in us who walk not after the flesh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “Do we then make void the law through faith?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: God forbid! yea, we establish the law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since the law was laboring at this, to make man righteous, but had not power, He came and brought in the way of righteousness by faith, and so established that which the law desired: and what the law could not by letters, this He accomplished by faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account He saith, “I am not come to destroy the law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if any one will inquire accurately, he will find also another, a third sense, in which this hath been done.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of what sort is it then?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the sense of that future code of laws, which He was about to deliver to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For His sayings were no repeal of the former, but a drawing out, and filling up of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, “not to kill,” is not annulled by the saying, Be not angry, but rather is filled up and put in greater security: and so of all the others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore, you see, as He had before unsuspectedly cast the seeds of this teaching; so at the time when from His comparison of the old and new commandments, He would be more distinctly suspected of placing them in opposition, He used His corrective beforehand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in a covert way He had indeed already scattered those seeds, by what He had said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, “Blessed are the poor,” is the same as that we are not to be angry; and, “Blessed are the pure in heart,” as not to “look upon a woman for lust;” and the “not laying up treasures on earth,” harmonizes with, “Blessed are the merciful;” and “to mourn” also, “to be persecuted” and “reviled,” coincide with “entering in at the strait gate;” and, “to hunger and thirst after righteousness,” is nothing else than that which He saith afterwards, “Whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye also to them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And having declared “the peace-maker blessed,” He again almost said the same, when He gave command “to leave the gift,” and hasten to reconciliation with him that was grieved, and about “agreeing with our adversary.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But there He set down the rewards of them that do right, here rather the punishments of them who neglect practice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore as in that place He said, “The meek shall inherit earth;” so here, “He who calleth his brother fool, shall be in danger of hell-fire;” and there, “The pure in heart shall see God;” here, he is a complete adulterer who looks unchastely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And having there called “the peace-makers, sons of God;” here He alarms us from another quarter, saying, “Lest at any time the adversary deliver thee to the judge.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus also, whereas in the former part He blesses them that mourn, and them that are persecuted; in the following, establishing the very same point, He threatens destruction to them that go not that way; for, “They that walk ‘in the broad way,’ saith He, ‘make their end there.’”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And, “Ye cannot serve God and mammon,” seems to me the same with, “Blessed are the merciful,” and, “those that hunger after righteousness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as I said, since He is going to say these things more clearly, and not only more clearly, but also to add again more than had been already said (for He no longer merely seeks a merciful man, but bids us give up even our coat; not simply a meek person, but to turn also the other cheek to him that would smite us): therefore He first takes away the apparent contradiction.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account, then, as I have already stated, He said this not once only, but once and again; in that to the words, “Think not that I am come to destroy,” He added, “I am not come to destroy, but to fulfill.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For verily I say unto you, Till Heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all come to pass.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now what He saith is like this: it cannot be that it should remain unaccomplished, but the very least thing therein must needs be fulfilled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which thing He Himself performed, in that He completedit with all exactness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And here He signifies to us obscurely that the fashion of the whole world is also being changed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nor did He set it down without purpose, but in order to arouse the hearer, and indicate, that He was with just cause introducing another discipline; if at least the very works of the creation are all to be transformed, and mankind is to be called to another country, and to a higher way of practising how to live.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called least in the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, having rid Himself of the evil suspicion, and having stopped the mouths of them who would fain gainsay, then at length He proceeds to alarm, and sets down a heavy, denunciation in support of the enactments He was entering on.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to His having said this in behalf not of the ancient laws, but of those which He was proceeding to enact, listen to what follows, “For I say unto you,” saith He, “Except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the Scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He were threatening with regard to the ancient laws, how said He, “except it shall exceed?” since they who did just the same as those ancients, could not exceed them on the score of righteousness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But of what kind was the required excess?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not to be angry, not even to look upon a woman unchastely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what cause then doth He call these commandments “least,” though they were so great and high?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because He Himself was about to introduce the enactment of them; for as He humbled Himself, and speaks of Himself frequently with measure, so likewise of His own enactments, hereby again teaching us to be modest in everything.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, since there seemed to be some suspicion of novelty, He ordered His discourse for a while with reserve.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when thou hearest, “least in the kingdom of Heaven,” surmise thou nothing but hell and torments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He was used to mean by “the kingdom,” not merely the enjoyment thereof, but also the time of the resurrection, and that awful coming.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how could it be reasonable, that while he who called his brother fool, and trangressed but one commandment, falls into hell; the breaker of them all, and instigator of others to the same, should be within the kingdom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This therefore is not what He means, but that such a one will be at that time least, that is, cast out, last.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And he that is last will surely then fall into hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, being God, He foreknew the laxity of the many, He foreknew that some would think these sayings were merely hyperbolical, and would argue about the laws, and say, What, if any one call another a fool, is he punished?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If one merely look on a woman, doth he become an adulterer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this very cause He, destroying such insolence beforehand, hath set down the strongest denunciation against either sort, as well them who transgress, as them who lead on others so to do.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowing then His threat as we do, let us neither ourselves transgress, nor discourage such as are disposed to keep these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But whosoever shall do and teach,” saith He, “shall be called great.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not to ourselves alone, should we be profitable, but to others also; since neither is the reward as great for him who guides himself aright, as for one who with himself adds also another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as teaching without doing condemns the teacher (for “thou which teachest another,” it is said, “teachest thou not thyself”?) so doing but not guiding others, lessens our reward.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: One ought therefore to be chief in either work, and having first set one’s self right, thus to proceed also to the care of the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For on this account He Himself hath set the doing before the teaching; to intimate that so most of all may one be able to teach, but in no other way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For one will be told, “Physician, heal thyself.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since he who cannot teach himself, yet attempts to set others right, will have many to ridicule him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather such a one will have no power to teach at all, his actions uttering their voice against him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if he be complete in both respects, “he shall be called great in the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For I say unto you, Except your righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the Scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here by righteousness He means the whole of virtue; even as also discoursing of Job, He said, “He was a blameless man, righteous.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: According to the same signification of the word, Paul also called that man “righteous” for whom, as he said, no law is even set.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For,” saith he, “a law is not made for a righteous man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in many other places too one might find this name standing for virtue in general.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But observe, I pray thee, the increase of grace; in that He will have His newly-come disciples better than the teachers in the old covenant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by “Scribes and Pharisees” here, He meant not merely the lawless, but the well-doers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, were they not doing well, He would not have said they have a righteousness; neither would He have compared the unreal to the real.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And observe also here, how He commends the old law, by making a comparison between it and the other; which kind of thing implies it to be of the same tribe and kindred.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For more and less, is in the same kind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He doth not, you see, find fault with the old law, but will have it made stricter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas, had it been evil, He would not have required more of it; He would not have made it more perfect, but would have cast it out.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And how one may say, if it be such, doth it not bring us into the Kingdom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It doth not now bring in them who live after the coming of Christ, favored as they are with more strength, and bound to strive for greater things: since as to its own foster-children, them it doth bring in one and all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for “many shall come,” saith He, “from east and west, and shall lie down in the bosoms of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Lazarus also receiving the great prize, is shown dwelling in Abraham’s bosom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And all, as many as have shone forth with excellency in the old dispensation, shone by it, every one of them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Christ Himself, had it been in anything evil or alien from Him, would not have fulfilled it all when He came.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if only to attract the Jews He was doing this, and not in order to prove it akin to the new law, and concurrent therewith; wherefore did He not also fulfill the laws and customs of the Gentiles, that He might attract the Gentiles also?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that from all considerations it is clear, that not from any badness in itself doth it fail to bring us in, but because it is now the season of higher precepts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if it be more imperfect than the new, neither doth this imply it to be evil: since upon this principle the new law itself will be in the very same case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because in truth our knowledge of this, when compared with that which is to come, is a sort of partial and imperfect thing, and is done away on the coming of that other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For when,” saith He, “that which is perfect is come, then that which is in part shall be done away:”even as it befell the old law through the new.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet we are not to blame the new law for this, though that also gives place on our attaining unto the Kingdom: for “then,” saith He, “that which is in part shall be done away:” but for all this we call it great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since then both the rewards thereof are greater, and the power given by the Spirit more abundant, in reason it requires our graces to be greater also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it is no longer “a land that floweth with milk and honey,” nor a comfortableold age, nor many children, nor corn and wine, and flocks and herds: but Heaven, and the good things in the Heavens, and adoption and brotherhood with the Only-Begotten, and to partake of the inheritance and to be glorified and to reign with Him, and those unnumbered rewards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as to our having received more abundant help, hear thou Paul, when he saith, “There is therefore no condemnation now to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit:for the law of the Spirit of life hath made me free from the law of sin and death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And now after threatening the transgressors, and setting great rewards for them that do right, and signifying that He justly requires of us something beyond the former measures; He from this point begins to legislate, not simply, but by way of comparison with the ancient ordinances, desiring to intimate these two things: first, that not as contending with the former, but rather in great harmony with them, He is making these enactments; next, that it was meet and very seasonable for Him to add thereto these second precepts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that this may be made yet clearer, let us hearken to the words of the Legislator.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then doth He Himself say?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Ye have heard that it was said to them of old time, Thou shalt not kill.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet it was Himself who gave those laws also, but so far He states them impersonally.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if on the one hand He had said, “Ye have heard that I said to them of old,” the saying would have been hard to receive, and would have stood in the way of all the hearers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If again, on the other hand, after having said, “Ye have heard that it was said to them of old by my Father,” He had added, “But I say,” He would have seemed to be taking yet more on Himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He hath simply stated it, making out thereby one point only; the proof that in fitting season He had come saying these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by the words, “It was said to them of old,” He pointed out the length of the time, since they received this commandment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He did to shame the hearer, shrinking from the advance to the higher class of His commandments; as though a teacher should say to a child that was indolent, “Knowest thou not how long a time thou hast consumed in learning syllables?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This then He also covertly intimates by the expression, “them of old time,” and thus for the future summons them on to the higher order of His instructions: as if He had said, “Ye are learning these lessons long enough, and you must henceforth press on to such as are higher than these.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is well that He doth not disturb the order of the commandments, but begins first with that which comes earlier, with which the law also began.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for this too suits with one showing the harmony between them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But I say unto you, that whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause, shall be in danger of the judgment.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou authority in perfection?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou a bearing suited to a legislator?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, which among prophets ever spake on this wise? which among righteous men? which among patriarchs?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: None; but, “Thus saith the Lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the Son not so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because they were publishing their Master’s commands, He His Father’s.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when I say, “His Father’s,” I mean His own.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For mine,” saith He, “are thine, and thine are mine.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they had their fellow-servants to legislate for, He His own servants.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us now ask those who reject the law, “is, ‘Be not angry’ contrary to ‘Do no murder’? or is not the one commandment the completion and the development of the other?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Clearly the one is the fulfilling of the other, and that is greater on this very account.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since he who is not stirred up to anger, will much more refrain from murder; and he who bridles wrath will much more keep his hands to himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wrath is the root of murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And you see that He who cuts up the root will much more remove the branches; or rather, will not permit them so much as to shoot out at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not therefore to abolish the law did He make these enactments, but for the more complete observation of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For with what design did the law enjoin these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was it not, that no one might slay his neighbor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It follows, that he who was opposing the law would have to enjoin murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to murder, were the contrary to doing no murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if He doth not suffer one even to be angry, the mind of the law is established by Him more completely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that studies to avoid murder will not refrain from it equally with him that hath put away even anger; this latter being further removed from the crime.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that we may convict them in another way also, let us bring forward all their allegations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then do they affirm?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They assert that the God who made the world, who “makes His sun to rise on the evil and on the good, who sends the rain on the just and on the unjust,” is in some sense an evil being.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But the more moderate (forsooth) among them, though declining this, yet while they affirm Him to be just, they deprive Him of being good.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And some other one, who is not, nor made any of the things that are, they assign for a Father to Christ.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And they say that he, who is not good, abides in his own, and preserves what are his own; but that He, that is good, seeks what are another’s, and desires of a sudden to become a Saviour to them whose Creator He was not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou the children of the devil, how they speak out of the fountain of their father, alienating the work of creation from God: while John cries out, “He came unto His own,” and, “The world was made by Him?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In the next place, they criticise the law in the old covenant, which bids put out “an eye for an eye,” and “a tooth for a tooth;”and straightway they insult and say, “Why, how can He be good who speaks so?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then do we say in answer to this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That it is the highest kind of philanthropy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He made this law, not that we might strike out one another’s eyes, but that fear of suffering by others might restrain us from doing any such thing to them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As therefore He threatened the Ninevites with overthrow, not that He might destroy them, (for had that been His will, He ought to have been silent), but that He might by fear make them better, and so quiet His wrath: so also hath He appointed a punishment for those who wantonly assail the eyes of others, that if good principle dispose them not to refrain from such cruelty, fear may restrain them from injuring their neighbors’ sight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if this be cruelty, it is cruelty also for the murderer to be restrained, and the adulterer checked.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these are the sayings of senseless men, and of those that are mad to the extreme of madness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For I, so far from saying that this comes of cruelty, should say, that the contrary to this would be unlawful, according to men’s reckoning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas, thou sayest, “Because He commanded to pluck out “an eye for an eye,” therefore He is cruel;” I say, that if He had not given this commandment, then He would have seemed, in the judgment of most men, to be that which thou sayest He is.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For let us suppose that this law had been altogether done away, and that no one feared the punishment ensuing thereupon, but that license had been given to all the wicked to follow their own disposition in all security, to adulterers, and to murderers,to perjured persons, and to parricides; would not all things have been turned upside down? would not cities, market-places, and houses, sea and land, and the whole world, have been filled with unnumbered pollutions and murders?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Every one sees it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if, when there are laws, and fear, and threatening, our evil dispositions are hardly checked; were even this security taken away, what is there to prevent men’s choosing vice? and what degree of mischief would not then come revelling upon the whole of human life?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The rather, since cruelty lies not only in allowing the bad to do what they will, but in another thing too quite as much; to overlook, and leave uncared for, him who hath done no wrong, but who is without cause or reason suffering ill.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me; were any one to gather together wicked men from all quarters, and arm them with swords, and bid them go about the whole city, and massacre all that came in their way, could there be anything more like a wild beast than he?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what if some other should bind, and confine with the utmost strictness those whom that man had armed, and should snatch from those lawless hands them, who were on the point of being butchered; could anything be greater humanity than this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now then, I bid thee transfer these examples to the law likewise; for He that commands to pluck out “an eye for an eye,” hath laid the fear as a kind of strong chain upon the souls of the bad, and so resembles him, who detains those assassins in prison; whereas he who appoints no punishment for them, doth all but arm them by such security, and acts the part of that other, who was putting the swords in their hands, and letting them loose over the whole city.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not, how the commandments, so far from coming of cruelty, come rather of abounding mercy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if on account of these thou callest the Lawgiver grievous, and hard to bear with; tell me which sort of command is the more toilsome and grievous, “Do no murder,” or, “Be not even angry”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which is more in extreme, he who exacts a penalty for murder, or for mere anger?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He who subjects the adulterer to vengeance after the fact, or he who enjoins a penalty even for the very desire, and that penalty everlasting?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See ye not how their reasoning comes round to the very contrary? how the God of the old covenant, whom they call cruel, will be found mild and meek: and He of the new, whom they acknowledged to be good, will be hard and grievous, according to their madness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas we say, that there is but one and the same Legislator of either covenant, who dispensed all meetly, and adapted to the difference of the times the difference between the two systems of law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither are the first commandments cruel, nor the second hard and grievous, but all of one and the same providential care.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For that He Himself gave the old covenant also, hear the affirmation of the prophet, or rather (so we must speak), of Him who is both the one and the other: “I will make a covenant with you, not according to the covenant which I made with your fathers.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if he receive not this, who is diseased with the Manichæan doctrines,let him hear Paul saying the very same in another place, “For Abraham had two sons, one by the bondmaid, and another by the freewoman; and these are two covenants.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As therefore in that case the wives are different, the husband the same; so here too the covenants are two, the Lawgiver one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And to prove to thee that it was of one and the same mildness; in the one He saith, “An eye for an eye,” but in this other,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “If one smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as in that case He checks him that doth the wrong with the fear of this suffering, even so also in this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “How so,” it may be said, “when He bids turn to him the other cheek also?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, what of that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since not to take away his fear did He enjoin this, but as charging yourself to allow him to take his fill entirely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Neither did He say, that the other continues unpunished, but, “do not thou punish;” at once both enhancing the fear of him that smiteth, if he persist, and comforting him who is smitten.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But these things we have said, as one might say them incidentally, concerning all the commandments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now we must go on to that which is before us, and keep to the thread of what had been affirmed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “He that is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment:” so He speaks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus He hath not altogether taken the thing away: first, because it is not possible, being a man, to be freed from passions: we may indeed get the dominion over them, but to be altogether without them is out of the question.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Next, because this passion is even useful, if we know how to use it at the suitable time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See, for instance, what great good was wrought by that anger of Paul, which he felt against the Corinthians, on that well-known occasion; and how, as it delivered them from a grievous pest, so by the same means again he recovered the people of the Galatians likewise, which had fallen aside; and others too beside these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is the proper time for anger?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When we are not avenging ourselves, but checking others in their lawless freaks, or forcing them to attend in their negligence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what is the unsuitable time?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When we do so as avenging ourselves: which Paul also forbidding, said “Avenge not yourselves, dearly beloved, but rather give place unto wrath.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When we are contending for riches: yea, for this hath he also taken away, where he saith, “Why do ye not rather take wrong? why do ye not rather suffer yourselves to be defrauded?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as this last sort is superfluous, so is the first necessary and profitable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But most men do the contrary; becoming like wild beasts when they are injured themselves, but remiss and cowardly when they see despite done to another: both which are just opposite to the laws of the Gospel.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Being angry then is not a transgression, but being so unseasonably.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause the prophet also said, “Be ye angry, and sin not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “And whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By the council in this place He means the tribunal of the Hebrews: and He hath mentioned this now, on purpose that He might not seem everywhere to play the stranger and innovator.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this word, “Raca,” is not an expression of a great insolence, but rather of some contempt and slight on the part of the speaker.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as we, giving orders either to our servants, or to any very inferior person, say, “Away with thee; you here, tell such an one:”so they who make use of the Syrians’ language say, “Raca,” putting that word instead of “thou.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But God, the lover of man, roots up even the least faults, commanding us to behave to one another in seemly manner, and with due respect; and this with a view of destroying hereby also the greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To many this commandment hath appeared grievous and galling, if for a mere word we are really to pay so great a penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And some even say that it was spoken rather hyperbolically.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But I fear lest, when we have deceived ourselves with words here, we may in deeds there suffer that extreme punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For wherefore, tell me, doth the commandment seem overburdensome?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowest thou not that most punishments and most sins have their beginning from words?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for by words are blasphemies, and denials are by words, and revilings, and reproaches, and perjuries, and bearing false witness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Regard not then its being a mere word, but whether it have not much danger, this do thou inquire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Art thou ignorant that in the season of enmity, when wrath is inflamed, and the soul kindled, even the least thing appears great, and what is not very reproachful is counted intolerable?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And often these little things have given birth even to murder, and overthrown whole cities.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For just as where friendship is, even grievous things are light, so where enmity lies beneath, very trifles appear intolerable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And however simply a word be spoken, it is surmised to have been spoken with an evil meaning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as in fire: if there be but a small spark, though thousands of planks lie by, it doth not easily lay hold of them; but if the flame have waxed strong and high, it readily seizes not planks only, but stones, and all materials that fall in its way; and by what things it is usually quenched, by the same it is kindled the more (for some say that at such a time not only wood and tow, and the other combustibles, but even water darted forth upon it doth but fan its power the more); so is it also with anger; whatever any one may say, becomes food in a moment for this evil conflagration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All which kind of evils Christ checking beforehand, had condemned first him that is angry without a cause to the judgment, (this being the very reason why He said, “He that is angry shall be in danger of the judgment”); then him that saith “Raca,” to the council.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But as yet these are no great things; for the punishments are here.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore for him who calleth “fool” He hath added the fire of hell, now for the first time mentioning the name of hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For having before discoursed much of the kingdom, not until then did He mention this; implying, that the former comes of His own love and indulgence towards man, this latter of our negligence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see how He proceeds by little and little in His punishments, all but excusing Himself unto thee, and signifying that His desire indeed is to threaten nothing of the kind, but that we drag Him on to such denunciations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For observe: “I bade thee,” saith He, “not be angry for nought, because thou art in danger of the judgment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou hast despised the former commandment: see what anger hath produced; it hath led thee on straightway to insult, for thou hast called thy brother ‘Raca.’

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, I set another punishment, ‘the council.’

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou overlook even this, and proceed to that which is more grievous, I visit thee no longer with these finite punishments, but with the undying penalty of hell, lest after this thou shouldest break fortheven to murder.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is nothing, nothing in the world more intolerable than insolence; it is what hath very great powerto sting a man’s soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when the word too which is spoken is in itself more wounding than the insolence, the blaze becomes twice as great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Think it not then a light thing to call another “fool.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when of that which separates us from the brutes, and by which especially we are human beings, namely, the mind and the understanding,—when of this thou hast robbed thy brother, thou hast deprived him of all his nobleness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not then regard the words merely, but realizing the things themselves, and his feeling, let us consider how great a wound is made by this word, and unto how much evil it proceeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause Paul likewise cast out of the kingdom not only “the adulterous” and “the effeminate,” but “the revilers”also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with great reason: for the insolent man mars all the beauty of charity, and casts upon his neighbor unnumbered ills, and works up lasting enmities, and tears asunder the members of Christ, and is daily driving away that peace which God so desires: giving much vantage ground unto the devil by his injurious ways, and making him the stronger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore Christ Himself, cutting out the sinews of the devil’s power, brought in this law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed He makes much account of love: this being above all things the mother of every good, and the badge of His disciples, and the bond which holds together our whole condition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With reason therefore doth He remove with great earnestness the roots and the sources of that hatred which utterly spoils it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Think not therefore that these sayings are in any wise hyperbolical, but consider the good done by them, and admire the mildness of these laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For there is nothing for which God takes so much pains, as this; that we should be united and knit together one with another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore both in His own person, and by His disciples, as well those in the Old, as in the New Testament, He makes so much account of this commandment; and is a severe avenger and punisher of those who despise the duty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in truth nothing so effectually gives entrance and root to all wickedness, as the taking away of love.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore He also said, “When iniquity abounds, the love of the many shall wax cold.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus Cain became his brother’s murderer; thus Esau; thus Joseph’s brethren; thus our unnumbered crimes have come revelling in, this bond being dissevered.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: You see why He Himself also roots out whatever things injure this, on every side, with great exactness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Neither doth He stop at those precepts only which have been mentioned, but adds also others more than those: whereby He signifies how much account He makes thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Namely, having threatened by “the council,” by “the judgment,” and by “hell,” He added other sayings again in harmony with the former, saying thus:

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “If thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath ought against thee; leave there thy gift before the altar, and go away;first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: O exceeding love to man!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He makes no account of the honor due unto Himself, for the sake of our love towards our neighbor; implying that not at all from any enmity, nor out of any desire to punish, had He uttered those former threatenings, but out of very tender affection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what can be milder than these sayings?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Let my service,” saith he, “be interrupted, that thy love may continue; since this also is a sacrifice, thy being reconciled to thy brother.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for this cause He said not, “after the offering,” or “before the offering;” but, while the very gift lies there, and when the sacrifice is already beginning, He sends thee to be reconciled to thy brother; and neither after removing that which lies before us,nor before presenting the gift, but while it lies in the midst, He bids thee hasten thither.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: With what motive then doth He command so to do, and wherefore?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These two ends, as it appears to me, He is hereby shadowing out and providing for.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: First, as I have said, His will is to point out that He highly values charity,and considers it to be the greatest sacrifice: and that without it He doth not receive even that other; next, He is imposing such a necessity of reconciliation, as admits of no excuse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whoso hath been charged not to offer before he be reconciled, will hasten, if not for love of his neighbor, yet, that this may not lie unconsecrated,to run unto him who hath been grieved, and do away the enmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He hath also expressed it all most significantly, to alarm and thoroughly to awaken him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, when He had said, “Leave thy gift,” He stayed not at this, but added, “before the altar” (by the very place again causing him to shudder); “and go away.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said not merely, “Go away,” but He added, “first, and then come and offer thy gift.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By all these things making it manifest, that this table receives not them that are at enmity with each other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let the initiated hear this, as many as draw nigh in enmity: and let the uninitiated hear too: yea, for the saying hath some relation to them also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For they too offer a gift and a sacrifice: prayer, I mean, and alms-giving.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to this also being a sacrifice, hear what the prophet saith: “A sacrifice of praise will glorify me;”and again, “Sacrifice to God a sacrifice of praise;”and, “The lifting up of mine hands is an evening sacrifice.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that if it be but a prayer, which thou art offering in such a frame of mind, it were better to leave thy prayer, and become reconciled to thy brother, and then to offer thy prayer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to this end were all things done: to this end even God became man, and took order for all those works, that He might set us at one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whereas in this place He is sending the wrong doer to the sufferer, in His prayer He leads the sufferer to the wrong doer, and reconciles them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as there He saith, “Forgive men their debts;” so here, “If he hath ought against thee, go thy way unto him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, even here too He seems to me to be sending the injured person: and for some such reason He said not, “Reconcile thyself to thy brother,” but, “Be thou rec onciled.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And while the saying seems to pertain to the aggressor, the whole of it really pertains to him that is aggrieved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, “If thou art reconciled to him,” saith Christ, “through thy love to him thou wilt have me also propitious, and wilt be able to offer thy sacrifice with great confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou art still irritated, consider that even I readily command that which is mine to be lightly esteemed, that ye may become friends; and let these thoughts be soothing to thine anger.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said not, “When thou hast suffered any of the greater wrongs, then be reconciled; but, “Though it be some trifle that he hath against thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He added not, “Whether justly or unjustly;” but merely, “If he hath ought against thee.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though it be justly, not even in that case oughtest thou to protract the enmity; since Christ also was justly angered with us, yet nevertheless He gave Himself for us to be slain, “not imputing those trespasses.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause Paul also, when urging us in another way to reconciliation, said, “Let not the sun go down upon your wrath.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For much as Christ by this argument of the sacrifice, so there Paul by that of the day, is urging us on to the self-same point.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because in truth he fears the night, lest it overtake him that is smitten alone, and make the wound greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For whereas in the day there are many to distract, and draw him off; in the night, when he is alone, and is thinking it over by himself, the waves swell, and the storm becomes greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore Paul, you see, to prevent this, would fain commit him to the night already reconciled, that the devil may after that have no opportunity, from his solitude, to rekindle the furnace of his wrath, and make it fiercer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus also Christ permits not, though it be ever so little delay, lest, the sacrifice being accomplished, such an one become more remiss, procrastinating from day to day: for He knows that the case requires very speedy treatment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as a skillful physician exhibits not only the preventives of our diseases, but their correctives also, even so doth He likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, to forbid our calling “fool,” is a preventive of enmity; but to command reconciliation is a means of removing the diseases that ensue on the enmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark how both commands are set forth with earnestness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as in the former case He threatened hell, so here He receives not the gift before the reconciliation, indicating great displeasure, and by all these methods destroying both the root and the produce.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And first of all He saith, “Be not angry;” and after that, “revile not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For indeed both these are augmented, the one by the other: from enmity is reviling, from reviling enmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: On this account then He heals now the root, and now the fruit; hindering indeed the evil from ever springing up in the first instance: but if perchance it may have sprouted up and borne its most evil fruit, then by all means He burns it down the more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, you see, having mentioned, first the judgment, then the council, then hell, and having spoken of His own sacrifice, He adds other topics again, thus speaking:

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Agree with thine adversary quickly, whilst thou art in the way with him.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That is, that thou mayest not say, “What then, if I am injured;” “what if I am plundered, and dragged too before the tribunal?” even this occasion and excuse He hath taken away: for He commands us not even so to be at enmity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, since this injunction was great, He draws His advice from the things present, which are wont to restrain the grosser sort more than the future.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Why, what sayest thou?” saith He.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “That thine adversary is stronger, and doeth thee wrong?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Of course then he will wrong thee more, if thou do not make it up, but art forced to go into court.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For in the former case, by giving up some money, thou wilt keep thy person free; but when thou art come under the sentence of the judge, thou wilt both be bound, and pay the utmost penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou avoid the contest there, thou wilt reap two good results: first, not having to suffer anything painful: and secondly, that the good done will be thereafter thine own doing, and no longer the effect of compulsion on his part.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if thou wilt not be ruled by these sayings, thou wrongest not him, so much as thyself.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see here also how He hastens him; for having said, “Agree with thine adversary,” He added, “quickly;” and He was not satisfied with this, but even of this quickness He hath required a further increase, saying, “Whilst thou art in the way with him;” pressing and hastening him hereby with great earnestness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For nothing doth so much turn our life upside down, as delay and procrastination in the performance of our good works.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this hath often caused us to lose all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore, as Paul for his part saith, “Before the sun set, do away the enmity;” and as He Himself had said above, “Before the offering is completed, be reconciled;” so He saith in this place also, “Quickly, whilst thou art in the way with him,” before thou art come to the doors of the court; before thou standest at the bar and art come to be thenceforth under the sway of him that judgeth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since, before entering in, thou hast all in thine own control; but if thou set thy foot on that threshold, thou wilt not by ever so earnest efforts be able to arrange thy matters at thy will, having come under the constraint of another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is it “to agree?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He means either, “consent rather to suffer wrong?” or, “so plead the cause, as if thou wert in the place of the other;” that thou mayest not corrupt justice by self-love, but rather, deliberating on another’s cause as thine own, mayest so proceed to deliver thy vote in this matter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if this be a great thing, marvel not; since with this view did He set forth all those His blessings, that having beforehand smoothed and prepared the hearer’s soul, he might render it apter to receive all His enactments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now some say that He obscurely signifies the devil himself, under the name of the adversary; and bids us have nothing of his, (for this, they say, is to “agree” with him): no compromise being possible after our departure hence, nor anything awaiting us, but that punishment, from which no prayers can deliver.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But to me He seems to be speaking of the judges in this world, and of the way to the court of justice, and of this prison.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For after he had abashed men by higher things, and things future, he alarms them also by such as are in this life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which thing Paul also doth, using both the future and the present to sway his hearer: as when, deterring from wickedness, he points out to him that is inclined to evil, the ruler armed: thus saying, “But if thou do that which is evil, be afraid; for he beareth not the sword in vain; for he is a minister of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, enjoining us to be subject unto him, he sets forth not the fear of God only, but the threatening also of the other party, and his watchful care.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For ye must needs be subject, not only for wrath, but also for conscience sake.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because the more irrational, as I have already said, are wont to be sooner corrected by these things, things which appear and are at hand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore Christ also made mention, not of hell only, but also of a court of justice, and of being dragged thither, and of the prison, and of all the suffering there; by all these means destroying the roots of murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he who neither reviles, nor goes to law, nor prolongs enmity, how will he ever commit murder?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that from hence also it is evident, that in the advantage of our neighbor stands our own advantage.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that agrees with his adversary, will benefit himself much more; becoming free, by his own act, from courts of law, and prisons, and the wretchedness that is there.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then be obedient to His sayings; let us not oppose ourselves, nor be contentious; for first of all, even antecedently to their rewards, these injunctions have their pleasure and profit in themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if to the more part they seem to be burdensome, and the trouble which they cause, great; have it in thy mind that thou art doing it for Christ’s sake, and the pain will be pleasant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we maintain this way of reckoning at all times, we shall experience nothing burdensome, but great will be the pleasure we reap from every quarter; for our toil will no longer seem toil, but by how much it is enhanced, so much the sweeter and pleasanter doth it grow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When therefore the custom of evil things, and the desire of wealth, keep on bewitching thee; do thou war against them with that mode of thinking which tells us, “Great is the reward we shall receive, for despising the pleasure which is but for a season;” and say to thy soul; “Art thou quite dejected because I defraud thee of pleasure?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, be of good cheer, for I am introducing thee into Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou doest it not for man’s sake, but for God’s.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be patient therefore a little while, and thou shalt see how great is the gain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Endure for the present life, and thou shalt receive an unspeakable confidence.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if we would thus discourse with our own soul, and not only consider that which is burdensome in virtue, but take account also of the crown that comes thereof, we shall quickly withdraw it from all wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if the devil, holding out pleasure for a season, but pain for ever, is yet strong, and prevails; seeing our case is just the reverse in these matters, the labor temporary, the pleasure and profit immortal, what plea shall we have, if we follow not virtue after so great encouragement?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, the object of our labors is enough to set against all, and our clear persuasion that for God’s sake we are enduring all this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if one having the king his debtor, thinks he hath sufficient security for all his life; consider how great will he be, who hath made the Gracious and Everlasting God a debtor to himself, for good deeds both small and great.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not then allege to me labors and sweats; for not by the hope only of the things to come, but in another way also, God hath made virtue easy, assisting us everywhere, and putting His hand to our work.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou wilt only contribute a little zeal, everything else follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For to this end He will have thee too to labor a little, even that the victory may be thine also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And just as a king would have his own son present indeed in the array; he would have him shoot with the bow,and show himself, that the trophy may be reckoned his, while he achieves it all Himself: even so doth God in our war against the devil: He requires of thee one thing alone, that thou show forth a sincere hatred against that foe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou contribute this to Him, He by Himself brings all the war to an end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though thou burn with anger, with desire of riches, with any tyrannical passion whatever; if He see thee only stripping thyself and prepared against it, He comes quickly to thee, and makes all things easy, and sets thee above the flame, as He did those children of old in the Babylonian furnace: for they too carried in with them nought but their good will.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that we also may extinguish all the furnace of disordered pleasure here, and so escape the hell that is there, let these each day be our counsels, our cares, and our practice, drawing towards us the favor of God, both by our full purpose concerning good works, and by our frequent prayers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus even those things which appear insupportable now, will be most easy, and light, and lovely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because, so long as we are in our passions, we think virtue rugged and morose and arduous, vice desirable and most pleasing; but if we would stand off from these but a little, then both vice will appear abominable and unsightly, and virtue easy, mild, and much to be desired.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this you may learn plainly from those who have done well.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hear, for instance, how of those passions Paul is ashamed, even after his deliverance from them, saying, “For what fruit had ye then in those things, whereof ye are now ashamed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But virtue, even after his labor, he affirms to be light, callingthe laboriousness of our affliction momentary and “light,” and rejoicing in his sufferings, and glorying in his tribulations, and taking a pride in the marks wherewith he had been branded for Christ’s sake.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that we too may establish ourselves in this habit, let us order ourselves each day by what hath been said, and “forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before, let us press on towards the prize of the high calling:”unto which God grant that we may all attain, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and power for ever and ever.
